idea.hook: The Solar System's MOST EXTREME mountain isn't on Earth!
facts: Earth's highest point, Mount Everest, stands at 8,848 meters (29,032 feet) above sea level.
The protoplanet Vesta, only about 525 km (326 miles) in diameter, has a colossal central peak in its Rheasilvia crater.
This peak towers an incredible 22 kilometers (14 miles) from its base to its summit.
idea.payoff: That's a mountain almost 2.5 times taller than Mount Everest, on a body smaller than some states!
idea.title: Asteroid's MONSTER Mountain! 🏔️☄️

scenes.text: Solar System's MOST EXTREME mountain ISN'T on Earth!
scenes.visual: Starts with Earth rotating, then quickly zooms out to reveal a vast starry space background as text appears.

scenes.text: Earth's highest point: Mount Everest - 8,848 m (29,032 ft)
scenes.visual: An animated infographic of Mount Everest appearing, with its height clearly labeled next to it against a subtle Earth curve.

scenes.text: Meet Vesta: A protoplanet only 525 km (326 miles) in diameter.
scenes.visual: A detailed 3D model of the protoplanet Vesta slowly rotating, showing its spherical shape. Its diameter is indicated by a text overlay.

scenes.text: Vesta's Rheasilvia Peak: An incredible 22 km (14 miles) tall!
scenes.visual: Zoom into Vesta's surface, focusing on the Rheasilvia crater and its central peak. A vertical arrow highlights the peak's height.

scenes.text: That's 2.5x TALLER than Everest! On a body smaller than some states!
scenes.visual: A split-screen comparison infographic: Everest on one side, Vesta's Rheasilvia Peak on the other, scaled to visually emphasize the 2.5x height difference. Vesta rotates slowly in the background.
